Notice Of Motion & Motion For Relief From Judgment (Memorandum Of Costs) And To Strike Or Tax Costs
Off Calendar Sep-10-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Jeffrey Workman for Plaintiff.
Sally Liu Defendant in propria persona.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Motion For Relief From Judgment (Memorandum Of Costs) And To Strike Or Tax Costs:
Defendant Sally Liu's motion for relief from judgment (memorandum of costs) and to strike or tax costs is denied. On February 21, 2020, Plaintiffs filed a memorandum of costs after judgment in the total amount of $2,195, and served it by mail on Defendant's then counsel of record, Glen Broemer. Defendant's motion to tax costs was required to be filed and served within 10 days after service of that cost memorandum, or by March 12, 2020. (Code Civ. Proc. 685.070(c).) On March 9, 2020, Defendant executed a substitution of counsel in which she agreed to represent herself, which was filed with the Court on March 11, 2020. Sixteen days later, on March 27, 2020, she filed the instant motion. "If no motion to tax costs is made within the time provided in subdivision (c), the costs claimed in the memorandum are allowed." (Code Civ. Proc. 685.070(d).) Contrary to Defendant's contention, the mandatory attorney affidavit of fault provision of Code of Civil Procedure section 473 does not apply to untimely motions to tax costs, as a costs order does not constitute either a "default" or a "judgment" for purposes of those provisions. (Douglas v. Willis (1994) 27 Cal.App.4th 287, 290-292.) Defendant does not explain in her declaration why she or her then counsel did not timely file the motion to tax costs, and fails to make a sufficient showing of mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect to warrant discretionary relief under section 473. (See id. [affirming order denying relief for parties' failure to timely file a motion to tax costs].)

order signed.

Off Calendar Sep-16-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Edward Higginbotham Esq. 415-581-0885 ext. 205 for Plaintiff.
Glen Broemer, Esq. 805-663-2654 for Defendant Sally Liu through Court Call.
Sally Liu, 415-635-1783 Defendant is also present.

RULING - A R G U E D. Defendant requests judicial notice. Plaintiff argues hourly rate. The Court overrules Defendant's objections and denies the request for a statement of decision. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFF JOSE CERON, MONICA MEDINA, BRIAN MEDINA, JUDY JUDKINS' Motion For Attorneys Fees and Costs after Appeal.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. Plaintiffs' motion for award of attorney's fees and costs after appeal is granted in the amount of $25,340.00 in attorney's fees and $489.95 in costs. On November 25, 2015, judgment pursuant to a jury award was entered against defendant Sally Liu in the total amount of $48,967.00. On the same date, the Court granted plaintiffs' motion for attorney's fees and costs against defendant pursuant to San Francisco Rent Ordinance section 37.10B, which provides, "a prevailing party shall be entitled to reasonable attorney's fees and costs pursuant to order of the court." The award was in the amount of $22,750 for attorney's fees and costs in the amount of $1,847. The Court of Appeal affirmed the judgment (No. A147803, unpub. opn. filed Mar. 28, 2019) and denied rehearing, and the California Supreme Court denied review. (No. S255641, June 12, 2019.) The Court takes judicial notice of the Court of Appeal's opinion and the Supreme Court's order denying defendant's petition for review pursuant to Evid. Code 451(a).

Plaintiffs seek compensation for their efforts in (i) responding to defendant's unsuccessful order for order directing filing of satisfaction of judgment and return of over-collected amounts; (ii) preparing a respondents' brief in an appeal filed by defendant in which she represented herself in pro per; (iii) reviewing defendant's reply brief and unsuccessful petitions for rehearing and petition for review; and (iv) preparing the instant motion. Both parties' discussion of the facts of other cases in which defendant was involved is irrelevant and inappropriate; the sole question before this Court is whether the amount of attorney's fees sought by plaintiffs in the instant case is reasonable. Based on its review of the record and of counsel's time records, the Court finds that the amount of time for which counsel seeks fees, 90.5 hours, is excessive for the quantity and nature of the work performed, and that calculation of a reasonable lodestar requires a 20% reduction, to 72.4 hours. The Court further finds that $350 per hour is a reasonable hourly rate for counsel's time. Counsel does not seek a multiplier, nor does the Court believe that one is appropriate.

Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

The Court notes the nature of the hearing and that it is a somewhat unusual motion, whereupon Mr. Haynes re-affirms the basis for the hearing, to whit: the trial was not reported and there is, as a consequence, no record on appeal. The Court gramts the motion.

The Court addresses post-hearing procedure, noting that the proposed record for settlement must be submitted to the Superior Court for approval and that the trial judge should ordinarily hear the matter. In the instant case, trial Judge Rebecca Wiseman was sitting by assignment, that assignment ended, and she and has since moved to Oregon. Mr. Haynes is instructed to submit the proposed record for settlement to Dept. 206 which will attempt to coordinate review of the proposed record with Judge Wiseman and, if possible, coordinate conference hearing with Judge Wiseman to confirm the settled record. The Court further notes that if Judge Wiseman is not available, another trial judge may be assigned to the hearing on settlement of the record.

Mr. Haynes represents to the Court that the proposed Order for today's hearing will be submitted today and the Court advises that the Order should be straightforward.
